text: The appendix is visibly adhered to the right pelvic brim, partially covered by the terminal ileum.
The surgeon examines the area to locate the base of the appendix, which is adhered to the right pelvic brim.
The surgeon observes the anti-mesenteric fat of the ileum and the cecum covering the base of the appendix, noting its retrocecal position.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the right colon and cecum, attempting to increase their mobility for better visualization of the surgical field.

The surgeon utilizes three laparoscopic ports: an umbilical Hassan port, a five-millimeter super pubic port, and a five-millimeter port in the left lower quadrant, providing access for the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon encounters difficulty as the cecum is adhered to the pelvic brim, hindering its mobilization from the sidewall.
The surgeon begins to chip away at the adhesions impacting the mobility of the cecum and right colon, using careful dissection techniques to free these structures from the pelvic brim.
The surgeon carefully applies blunt traction to mobilize the right colon and cecum, ensuring the ureter remains distant and unaffected by the manipulation.
The surgeon lifts the rind covering the appendix to improve visibility of the underlying structures.
The surgeon opens the roof of the appendix, carefully lifting the covering tissue to expose the underlying structures.
The surgeon exposes the necrotic and inflamed wall of the appendix body, revealing its compromised condition.
The surgeon attempts to further mobilize the appendix, manipulating it to improve visibility and access for the procedure.
Pus begins to emerge from the necrotic appendix as the surgeon continues the procedure.
The surgeon attempts to improve the view of the appendix base, which remains partially obscured.
The surgeon cleans additional pus from the surgical field, focusing on the area around the necrotic appendix.
The surgeon opens the area to improve visibility and access, likely to further expose the appendix or surrounding structures.
The surgeon enters the natural tissue plane, revealing inflamed tissue that begins to separate more easily.
The surgeon mobilizes the cecum, adjusting its position to improve access and visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon observes an inflammatory reaction extending into the retroperitoneum, with visible signs of inflammation in the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon gains mobility to lift the cecum, aiming to visualize the retroperitoneal attachment where the appendix connects to the cecum.
The surgeon works on the medial surface of the cecum, searching for the anti-mesenteric fat to approach the base of the appendix from this side.
The surgeon changes the approach to begin taking the mesoappendix, which involves manipulating the tissue surrounding the appendix to isolate it from the cecum.
The surgeon manipulates the cecum to access the base of the appendix, aiming to locate the open lumen for amputation. The plan includes suturing the hole closed if the cecum appears suitable.
The surgeon carefully dissects the mesoappendix, ensuring not to involve the terminal ileum or cecum during the procedure.
The surgeon enters a clear dissection plane, causing the appendix to disintegrate, revealing fecal material within the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the appendix along with visible fecal matter, ensuring careful separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies an open lumen in the cecum, indicating the location where the appendix was attached.
The surgeon observes the residual rind of the appendix, noting its proximity to the insertion point of the terminal ileum into the cecum.
The surgeon observes the cecum, noting its ratty appearance and the visible opening at the location where the appendix was previously attached.
The surgeon attempts to remove part of the wall to access healthy cecum for placing sutures, ensuring not to impinge upon the terminal ileum while securing the sutures to robust cecal tissue.
The surgeon examines the defect in the cecum, which is visible adjacent to the insertion point of the terminal ileum.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, carefully separating tissue layers to access the appendix and surrounding structures.
The surgeon displays the window created by the mobilization of the terminal ileum mesentery and the cecum, showing the exposed area in the surgical field.
The surgeon isolates the ileocolic pedicle, preparing it for further dissection or ligation.
The surgeon upsizes the suprapubic port to twelve millimeters to accommodate the stapling device.
The surgeon separates the ileocolic artery and vein, preparing the area for extraction.
The surgeon identifies the vein, likely the ileocolic vein, within the surgical field.
The surgeon is taking the small bowel mesentery, likely using surgical instruments to separate and manage the mesenteric tissue.
The surgeon uses a ligature to manage the thick and inflamed tissue, likely resulting from an inflammatory reaction, ensuring effective control of the tissue.
The surgeon continues to separate the ileocolic artery and vein, extending the dissection up to the bowel wall.
The surgeon carefully dissects the colon mesentery, ensuring preservation of the remaining mesentery while separating it from adjacent structures.
The surgeon focuses on the ileocolic region, likely involving the ileocolic artery and vein, as part of the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon dissects above the cecum at the junction with the ascending colon, carefully taking down the overlying lateral attachments.
The surgeon divides the terminal ileum using a stapler inserted through the suprapubic port, preparing for resection.
The surgeon mobilizes a portion of the hepatic flexure before using a stapler to divide the cecum and ascending colon.
The surgeon staples the colon from the umbilical port, with the camera positioned at the suprapubic port, providing a better angle for the procedure.
The surgeon performs an additional firing of the stapler at the junction of the cecum and ascending colon, using the suprapubic port for access.
The surgeon places the resected specimen above the liver for temporary storage.
The camera focuses on the colon, providing a clear view of its current state following previous surgical manipulations.
The surgeon prepares for an isoperistaltic anastomosis by creating an enterotomy and a colotomy, aligning the intestinal segments for connection.
The surgeon inserts the stapler through the suprapubic port, preparing to staple the colon.
The surgeon uses a stay suture to pull the bowel onto the stapler, aligning the anti-mesenteric sides to create a common channel.
The surgeon closes the common channel using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, securing the tissue with precise stitching.
The surgeon inspects the staple line, confirming there is no visible bleeding from the area.
The surgical field shows the area where the appendix was previously adhered, with surrounding tissues now visible after dissection.
The surgeon performs a quick visual inspection of the ureter to ensure it is intact and not damaged during the procedure.
The surgeon prepares to extract the appendix specimen by upsizing the suprapubic port to approximately two and a half to three centimeters.
